data.title: Yield curve trades surge as long-term Treasuries take hit from tariffs
data.description:
A popular trade in the U.S. Treasury market is experiencing a historic upswing as investors pull away from long-term government bonds in response to the intensifying trade conflict under President Donald Trump. The move reflects growing skepticism about Treasuries’ role as a safe haven during market turmoil.

Yields on long-dated bonds jumped last week, even as equity markets swung wildly. This has fueled bets on a steeper yield curve — particularly the widening spread between two-year and 30-year Treasuries — which has risen for nine consecutive weeks, matching a record dating back to 1992, Bloomberg News reported on Sunday.

A Treasury market steepener is a trading strategy that speculates the yield curve will steepen — meaning the difference between short-term and long-term U.S. Treasury yields will increase.

Asset managers like DoubleLine have benefited from positioning for such a shift, favoring trades that bet on further steepening.

Reasons for the selloff

The selloff in long-term bonds has been driven in part by concerns that the trade war, paired with rising fiscal deficits and the potential for more tax cuts, could reduce foreign demand for U.S. debt. Meanwhile, shorter-term bonds held up better amid expectations that the Federal Reserve may soon lower interest rates in response to a weakening economic outlook.

Some investors, including DoubleLine’s Bill Campbell, see the two-to-30-year curve as the most effective way to capture this trend.

At the same time, other traders suggest that recent volatility in long-term debt may also stem from hedge funds unwinding leveraged positions or banks raising cash to meet liquidity needs as the corporate outlook worsens.

Amid these developments, some Wall Street analysts are speculating about potential intervention by the Fed if bond markets become disorderly. Boston Fed President Susan Collins indicated the central bank would be prepared to step in if necessary.

Still, the steepener trade remains volatile. The yield curve narrowed sharply on Friday, and some firms, like BMO Capital Markets, took the opportunity to bet on a flatter curve in the short term.

Monitoring budget deficit

Market participants are keeping a close eye on fiscal developments. With the House advancing Trump’s proposed tax cuts, concerns are growing that an increase in the deficit could further strain demand for Treasuries. J.P. Morgan’s Priya Misra noted that a larger fiscal package would worsen the supply-demand balance for U.S. debt.

An upcoming $13 billion auction of 20-year Treasuries will provide the next indication of investor appetite, following mixed reactions to recent 10- and 30-year offerings.

Looking ahead, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is set to speak Wednesday, after previously signaling that the Fed would not respond hastily to trade-related developments. New York Fed President John Williams also highlighted the negative economic implications of Trump’s tariff policies, including slower growth, rising unemployment, and higher inflation.

Traders now expect the Fed to respond with three rate cuts by the end of the year as concerns of a sharp downturn continue to mount.

data.title: Netflix is testing OpenAI-powered search: report
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) has begun testing new search technology that is powered by OpenAI, Bloomberg reported Friday. 

The OpenAI-powered search engine enables users to search for shows using far more specific terms, such as the customer's mood, the report said. The feature will allow subscribers to make queries that go beyond genres or actors' names.

The new search engine is already available to some customers in Australia and New Zealand on iOS devices, with it expected to expand to several markets, including the U.S., the report said.

data.title: No plans for Trump-Xi talks as U.S.-China trade tensions grow, Greer says
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping currently "don't have any plans" to speak, U.S. Trade Representative Ambassador Jamieson Greer said Sunday amid concerns about the growing trade dispute between the two economic giants.

"Since April 2, we have this at the leaders level, and at some point, as President Trump has pointed out, we expect that we'll be able to have a conversation with them," Greer said during an interview on CBS's "Face the Nation."

President Trump declared April 2 “Liberation Day,” announcing a new tariff regime that includes a 10% baseline duty on imports from all countries, and higher “reciprocal” rates on nations that tax American goods.

Trump blinks on tariffs

Though the tariffs took effect, the administration paused most of them for 90 days after markets reacted sharply. Still, tariffs on Chinese imports were raised significantly, with a 125% rate and an additional 20% levy on fentanyl, bringing the total to 145%.

In response, China said Friday it would increase tariffs on U.S. goods from 84% to 125%. Chinese officials criticized the U.S. actions as violations of international trade norms and dismissed the new tariff levels as a “joke.”

Addressing the deteriorating relationship, Greer argued that the U.S. is taking broader steps to restore domestic manufacturing and trade independence. He said "the only reason we're really in this position right now is because China chose to retaliate."

"So many other countries affirmatively said they did not want to retaliate, 'we want to negotiate with the Americans,'" Greer said. "And the Chinese made a different decision … It was a Chinese decision. They have agency here."

Will China sell U.S. assets?

When asked about the possibility that China could begin selling its U.S.-based assets, Greer pointed to America’s longstanding dependence on Chinese supply chains and limited access to Chinese markets.

"We see China taking action, implementing their policy to try to be less tied up with us as well. So it's not surprising to see them making these kinds of moves," Greer said, adding that "we obviously have to be prepared on our part to have a resilient economy."

Regarding the 90-day tariff pause and whether that provides enough time to reach agreements with dozens of trading partners, Greer said discussions began before the tariffs were formally announced. "Because everyone knew tariffs were coming."

"The reality is, we're working around the clock, day and night, sharing paper, receiving offers and giving feedback to these countries with respect to how they can better achieve reciprocal trade with the United States," Greer said.

data.title: Bridgewater's Ray Dalio says trade war has brought US close to recession
data.description:
Ray Dalio, billionaire founder of Bridgewater Associates, said on Sunday the ongoing trade war had brought the United States close to recession. 

"I think that right now we are at a decision-making point and very close to a recession," said Dalio in an interview with NBC. "And I'm worried about something worse than a recession, if this isn't handled well." 

Dalio, who manages one of the world's largest hedge funds, was responding to a question about whether the U.S. would slip into recession due to President Donald Trump's tariff policies. 

Earlier in the month, Trump announced hefty reciprocal tariffs on almost every country on the planet, sending global markets spinning. Earlier this week, the President announced a 90-day pause on the levies, for everyone except China. On Friday night, the administration announced exemptions for popular electronic items mostly imported from China. 

Dalio highlighted the combination of tariffs, growing debt, and geopolitical tension as "very, very disruptive."

"We are having profound changes in our domestic order ... and we're having profound changes in the world order, such times are very much like the 1930s," said Dalio. "I've studied history, and this repeats over and over again."

Offering solutions, Dalio suggested reducing the deficit to 3% of the gross domestic product. "If they don't, we're going to have a supply-demand problem for debt at the same time as we have these other problems, and the results of that will be worse than a normal recession." 

Noting the financial crises of 1971 and 2008, Dalio said the current situation "could be more severe than those if these other matters simultaneously occur." 

Bridgewater, which was founded about 50 years ago and has 175 investors, correctly anticipated the 2008 financial crisis.

data.title: Top tech gainers and losers this week as tariffs continue to rattle markets: Broadcom leads, Enphase Energy lags
data.description:
• Wall Street’s euphoria disappeared soon after reaching historic highs earlier this week as U.S. President Donald Trump announced a pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China.

China, in retaliation, increased its tariffs on U.S. goods to 125% and stated it will disregard any additional tariff hikes announced by Washington moving forward.

The impact of the trade-war escalation was felt the most by semiconductor stocks, as some analysts expressed concern that tariffs have upended the global tech supply chain, which could lead to many tech companies withholding guidance in the upcoming earnings season. 

Despite a choppy week, the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK), which tracks the S&P 500’s Information Technology sector, gained more than 5% this week as a pause in tariffs reinstated some investor confidence. 

Let’s take a look at this week’s gainers and losers in the tech industry: 

Gainers: 

  • Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) +23.20% : The chipmaker’s stock advanced after it announced a share repurchase program of up to $10B, noting that the move underscores the board’s confidence in Broadcom’s robust cash flow and commitment to returning value to shareholders.
  • Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) +18.48% : Palantir shares continued their rally throughout the week, helped by Trump’s pause in tariffs.
  • Crowdstrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) +16.82%
  • Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) +16.64% : It was reported this week that the White House had stepped back from banning all of Nvidia's H20 GPUs to China, which had previously been under consideration.
  • KLA Corp (NASDAQ:KLAC) +16.52%: Citi maintained KLA Corp as one of its top picks while updating its Wafer Fab Equipment sector's outlook. Analysts said that while semi caps have relatively low 10% to 20% U.S. sales exposure and have been diversifying away from China in recent years, the impact of new tariffs in the rest of Asia impacts them.

Losers: 

  • Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) -9.73%
  • First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) -2.58%
  • Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) -1.31% : China's import tariffs on U.S. goods provide an exemption for U.S. semiconductor firms that outsource their manufacturing. Intel, which operates its own U.S.-based fabs - may be classified as U.S. origin and be liable for tariff rates of 84% or higher.
  • Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN) -2.15% : Texas Instruments also operates its own U.S.-based fabs, making it liable for higher tariff rates from China.
  • Accenture (NYSE:ACN) -0.42%

U.S. Tech-based ETFs to track:  (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NYSEARCA:VGT), (XLK), (NASDAQ:SMH), (NYSEARCA:IYW), (NYSEARCA:FTEC), (NASDAQ:SOXX), (BATS:IGV), (NASDAQ:CIBR), (NYSEARCA:IGM), (NYSEARCA:IXN).

data.title: Most Americans Trump tariffs will benefit corporations, wealthy: poll
data.description:
Most Americans say corporations and the wealthy will see the benefits of President Donald Trump’s tariff policies, according to a poll by CBS News/YouGov released Sunday.

According to the survey of 2,410 adults April-11, 74% said the tariffs and other trade policies would benefit wealthy people, while 71% felt they would help big corporations. Half said the American auto industry would benefit. 

Some 42% said the middle and working class would see benefits, with 45% saying labor unions would benefit.

Among Republicans, 91% believe that Trump has a plan with tariffs, 84% of Democrats and 57% of independents saying that he does not, the poll showed. 

Some 54% of respondents say Trump’s policies are responsible for the state of the economy after less than 90 days in office, and 35% say the economy is fairly or very good. 

Forty-nine percent say Trump’s policies are making them worse off, up from 42% last month. 

Three-quarters of respondents expect prices to rise in the short term because of the tariffs, while 42% say they will make the economy worse. Some 23% don’t expect a significant long-term impact on the economy, and 34% say they will improve the economy, according to the poll.  

Eighty-five percent of Republicans said they believe the tariffs will add jobs to the economy, compared with 43% of independents and 20% of Democrats. 

Most do not expect the tariffs to be permanent, with 59% saying that Trump will remove them eventually. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the fiancial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Commerce Department to launch Section 232 into semiconductors
data.description:
President Donald Trump is set to instruct the Commerce Department to initiate an investigation that could lead to fresh tariffs on semiconductors, Politico reported Sunday, citing an unnamed White House official. 

The investigation will fall under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which enables the President to restrict imports deemed a threat to national security, the report said. The goal of the investigation is to "revive U.S. manufacturing in critical technologies," the report added. 

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said on Sunday the administration's decision to exclude some electronic items from reciprocal tariffs was just a temporary reprieve, with the items expected to come under "semiconductor tariffs" in "a month or two." 

Trump on Saturday said he will provide an update on the administration's proposed semiconductor tariffs on Monday.

The President has previously suggested tariffs in the "in the neighborhood of 25%" on autos and similar duties on semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports.

More on Semiconductors

data.title: Biggest weekly communication services gainers and losers: DoorDash takes lead, Paramount Global top decliner
data.description:
Wall Street ended a turbulent week on a high note as markets continued reacting to President Donald Trump's tariff announcements. Earlier, investor sentiment improved after Trump declared a 90-day pause on planned reciprocal tariffs affecting over 75 countries. Despite the temporary relief, trade tensions with China remained elevated amid an ongoing tit-for-tat conflict between the two nations.
 

Overall, the sector, represented by the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLC), rose 4.5%, while the broader market (S&P 500) added 6%.

Weekly top 5 gainers

  • DoorDash (DASH): +10.6% DoorDash will begin offering sidewalk robot delivery in select U.S. cities through an expansion of its partnership with food delivery robotics company, Coco Robotics.
  • News Corporation (NWS) +9.2%
  • Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) +9%
  • Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG) +7.9% Google unveiled Gemini 2.5 Flash, a new AI large language model, and introduced its seventh-generation tensor processing unit chips. CEO Sundar Pichai reaffirmed Alphabet’s commitment to invest $75B in expanding its data center infrastructure. Google also confirmed layoffs affecting hundreds of employees in its Android, Pixel, and Chrome divisions.
  • Meta Platforms (META) +7.7%

Weekly top 5 losers

  • Paramount Global (PARA) -3.4% The $8B merger agreement between Paramount Global and Skydance Media has been extended by 90 days to July 7, 2025.
  • Fox Corporation (FOXA) -1.7%
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) -1.2% John Malone to step down from WBD's board; takes on 'chair emeritus' role.
  • Charter Communications (CHTR) -1.2%
  • Match Group (MTCH) -0.9%

ETFs to tab the communication services sector include: (NYSEARCA:XLC), (VOX), (IYZ), (RSPC), and (XTL).

data.title: Fed's Kashkari urges focus on inflation expectations amid market volatility
data.description:
Neel Kashkari, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, expressed confidence that financial markets will continue to operate smoothly despite recent volatility stemming from President Donald Trump’s evolving trade policies.

Speaking on CBS’s Face the Nation on Sunday, Kashkari emphasized the central bank’s commitment to maintaining stable inflation expectations as its primary objective.

Following a notable rise in 10-year Treasury yields last week, Kashkari said the direction of long-term interest rates will be shaped largely by U.S. trade and fiscal decisions.

“At the Fed, our job is to keep inflation under control so that rate isn’t even higher,” he said.

Kashkari was among several Federal Reserve officials who, in recent remarks, indicated support for keeping interest rates unchanged. Policymakers appear cautious about responding to tariff-related economic uncertainty with rate cuts, particularly if inflation pressures persist.

Commenting on the broader market outlook, Kashkari acknowledged that investors are working to understand the implications of a shifting U.S. policy landscape.

“Investors in the U.S. and around the world are trying to determine what is the new normal in America,” he said, while also underscoring that the Fed cannot influence the ultimate outcome of those policy shifts.

“Our role is to anchor inflation expectations and manage fluctuations along the way,” he added.

When asked whether markets remain orderly, Kashkari affirmed that they do, describing recent turbulence as a natural response to uncertainty: “But markets are functioning, trades are happening and so I anticipate that’s going to continue.”

data.title: Which stocks are most liquid? Goldman Sachs lists 50 of them
data.description:
The performance gap between high- and low-liquidity stocks has remained surprisingly stable despite recent market turbulence and worsening liquidity conditions, David Kostin, chief U.S. equity strategist at Goldman Sachs, said in an April 11 report.

Typically, in times of market stress, highly liquid stocks are among the first to be sold as investors look to reduce risk quickly. This dynamic often causes these stocks to underperform during broad market drawdowns. However, Kostin noted that the current environment is proving to be an exception.

The high liquidity basket tracked by Goldman has demonstrated resilience, thanks in part to the strong fundamental characteristics of its constituents. These companies generally exhibit high quality metrics, such as robust balance sheets, steady earnings growth and strong returns on equity — all factors that may have helped support valuations even as market volatility surged.

Goldman maintains two custom equity baskets, each with 50 stocks from the Russell 1000 Index, to monitor this trend. The firm rebalanced both baskets, including the High Liquidity (GSTHLLIQ) portfolio below, to reflect the evolving market landscape.

The illiquidity ratio measures how trading volumes affect a stock's price. In this case, it's the one-month average of the daily absolute value of returns divided by the dollars of trading volumes.

Goldman’s list of most illiquid stocks can be found at this link.
